g.data-narration: Welcome to learning about Software Interrupt Instructions in 8051 Microcontrollers. This is a core topic for embedded systems programming.
A software interrupt is a deliberate invocation through an instruction that suspends normal execution and transfers control to a service routine.
g.text: A software interrupt is triggered by executing a specific instruction in the program,
temporarily suspending normal execution to invoke an Interrupt Service Routine (ISR).
Used for: System calls, debugging, error handling, RTOS context switching

g.data-narration: Software interrupts are synchronous (triggered by code) while hardware interrupts are asynchronous (triggered by external events like pins).
ACALL is a 2-byte instruction that calls a subroutine within a 2KB page of program memory, taking 2 machine cycles.
g.text: • Address Range: Within 2KB page (±1KB from current PC)
• Stack: Pushes PC (low byte first, then high byte)
• Operation: PC ← PC + 2, then push PC, then PC ← addr

g.data-narration: LCALL is a 3-byte instruction that can call any subroutine in the entire 64KB program memory, taking 3 machine cycles.
g.text: • Address Range: Anywhere in 64KB program memory
• Stack: Pushes PC (low byte first, then high byte)
• Operation: PC ← PC + 3, then push PC, then PC ← 16-bit addr

g.data-narration: AJMP and LJMP are jump instructions used with interrupt vectors to redirect program flow to ISR locations in memory.
g.text: AJMP (Absolute Jump): 2-byte instruction, jumps within 2KB page
LJMP (Long Jump): 3-byte instruction, jumps anywhere in 64KB
Both used in interrupt vector tables to route to appropriate ISR
Example: ORG 0003H → LJMP EXT0_ISR → routes to external interrupt 0 handler

g.data-narration: When a software interrupt occurs, the microcontroller automatically pushes PC onto stack (low byte first, then high byte).
g.text: 1. Low byte of PC pushed first
2. High byte of PC pushed second

g.data-narration: The 8051 has fixed interrupt vector addresses: External0 at 0003H, Timer0 at 000BH, External1 at 0013H, Timer1 at 001BH, Serial at 0023H.
Always use RETI to return from an ISR - it restores PC and re-enables the interrupt system. Using RET causes unpredictable behavior.
Always save and restore ACC, PSW, and other modified registers in ISRs to preserve the calling program's state and prevent data corruption.
g.text: Critical rule: Save ALL registers modified in the ISR before using them, then restore them before RETI

g.data-narration: Software interrupts are used for system calls, debugging breakpoints, RTOS context switching, error handling, and accessing memory-mapped peripherals.
This example demonstrates using LCALL to invoke a software interrupt routine that saves registers, performs operations, and uses RETI to return.
ACALL uses 2KB range with 2 bytes and 2 cycles, while LCALL uses full 64KB with 3 bytes and 3 cycles - choose based on address needs.
Key exam points: Memorize vector addresses, always use RETI in ISRs, save registers, and understand ACALL vs LCALL differences.
g.text: ✓ Always use RETI in ISRs, not RET
✓ Save/restore ACC and PSW in ISRs

g.data-narration: In summary, software interrupts provide controlled transfer to ISRs using LCALL/ACALL instructions with proper stack management and register saving.
g.text: Summary: Master software interrupts for embedded systems success!
